seg_1.text: So before the break, we have mentioned that you should try to train a model with two layers and a model with six layers.

seg_2.text: And one thing that you may have observed is that a six-layer model may not be as good as a simple model.

seg_3.text: And this is weird because we have mentioned that deep neural network becomes powerful because we have more layers.

seg_4.text: But now this is not the case.

seg_5.text: So in the rest of this lecture, we are going to discuss several practical aspects in training neural network models.

seg_6.text: So let's start with the easy thing.

seg_7.text: Let's consider a practical scenario in our real life.

seg_8.text: That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.

seg_9.text: So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate this problem and you are given the files of 100 applicants.

seg_10.text: Among these 100 applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.

seg_11.text: So your goal is to build a model and predict who else is going to default.

seg_12.text: For such a job, what you may find is they give you some photos and they find that all the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews.

seg_13.text: And your model also find that blue shirts is a strong signal for default.

seg_14.text: So whether this is a valid feature or whether there are some problem when we make this conclusion.

seg_15.text: This is a very classic problem of overfitting or there's no codal evidence that blue shirts actually predict applicant's default behavior, right?

seg_16.text: The reason is because all of them are wearing blue shirts.

seg_17.text: So you cannot say only those who default wear such a blue shirts.

seg_19.text: And the other thing is using human logic, we know that the color of the shirts should have no codal inference with whether he's going to default or not.

seg_20.text: So because of that, we know that this feature may not be a valid feature.

seg_21.text: And this introduced our classic discussion of training error and generalization error.

seg_22.text: training error means the error on the training data set.

seg_23.text: And generalization error refers to your error on a new data.

seg_24.text: And we always say that the performance on the training data does not represent the performance on a new data set.

seg_25.text: This is very similar to we make practices for a future exam with past exams.

seg_26.text: For those who do well on past exam, that means you get a very small training error that does not guarantee a good score on the future exam, where the future exam actually represent the generalization error, right?

seg_27.text: And one student may get zero error on past exam by root learning.

seg_28.text: That means he remember all the answers mechanically.

seg_29.text: But that student may have a worse score compared to another student who understand the reasons for a given number of answers.

seg_30.text: So even if student B may not have good score on past exam, may not that good at past exam, as long as he can understand the reasonings, he should be able to have a higher score.

seg_31.text: So this is the trade-off we have mentioned in your Model 3 class about training and generalization or they call it bias and virus in some cases, right?

seg_32.text: So because of that, we need to evaluate our model to understand whether it has a good generalization performance.

seg_33.text: So in this process, we say that we can have two data sets.

seg_34.text: One data set is called validation data set.

seg_35.text: This is the data used to evaluate the model.

seg_36.text: And this data should not be mixed with your training data.

seg_37.text: And the validation data set will be used to do model selection, which model should I train.

seg_38.text: The test data set will be the data set that to evaluate your final performance.

seg_39.text: So that could be a future exam or the health cell price I bid it or the data set we use to determine the final winner on a Kaggle computation that is called private leaderboard.

seg_40.text: So in your homework 2, you will actually have such a test data set.

seg_41.text: So I'm sure you have learned how to use training validation and test the procedure in the machine learning course.

seg_42.text: , you may have learned I should split the data 70-30.

seg_43.text: This is the most classic way.

seg_44.text: And you have also learned about k-fold cross-validation.

seg_45.text: However, all of them are not applicable for deep learning models.

seg_46.text: So k-fold cross-validation is too expensive because you have known that training a deep learning model should take hours or even millions of hours.

seg_47.text: So because of that, it is impossible to just determine which model structure is better using k-fold.

seg_48.text: On the other hand, splitting the data in a 70-30 proportion may also not a good idea because now I have a very large data.

seg_49.text: I may have 1 million or even 1 billion data points.

seg_50.text: In that case, using 30% of the data to be validation or testing data may be a waste of your data.

seg_51.text: So because of that, for deep learning, people usually leave 10,000 or 1,000 data points as testing data and use all the other data as training data.

seg_52.text: So the percentage of training validation and testing could be very extreme.

seg_53.text: It could be 98% for training and 1% for validation and test.

seg_54.text: Or even if you have really 1 million data, then I will only leave 5,000 for validation and testing and use all the 99.5% of the data to train the model.

seg_55.text: So this is a difference between traditional machine learning model training as well as deep learning model training.

seg_56.text: And here we have mentioned two concepts.

seg_59.text: Parameters in neural networks refers to the weight and bias.

seg_60.text: Hyperparameters are the parameters that you are going to choose using model selection.

seg_61.text: And your model selection criteria is the performance on the validation data set.

seg_62.text: So far, we have mentioned so many hyperparameters learning rate, epochs, batch size, hidden layers, hidden units, so on and so forth.

seg_63.text: All of them should be determined using the validation data set.

seg_64.text: So how to decide what is the best combination of hyperparameters?

seg_65.text: , when we train deep learning models, this process is a very empirical process.

seg_66.text: So some people actually compare training a deep learning model with alchemy because people believe that there are so many hidden secrets that human cannot explain why on earth.
